ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a fried tofu class; pork floss topping is not separately encoded. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried tofu; pork floss component not separately represented in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for deep-fried tofu with pork floss as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of deep-fried tofu with pork floss is approximately 250 g, providing around 580 kcal, 35 g protein, 40 g fat, and 20 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tofu, fried (SR Legacy FDC ID 172443) and Pork, cured, dried product (SR Legacy); composite estimate weighted approximately 70% fried tofu / 30% pork floss by mass
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for deep-fried tofu with pork floss as a composite dish. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: fried tofu (FDC ID 172443, approximately 70% weight) and dried/cured pork product approximating pork floss (approximately 30% weight). Input values from GrabFood (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with minor rounding. Energy cross-check: 14 g protein × 4 + 16 g fat × 9 + 8 g carbohydrate × 4 = 56 + 144 + 32 = 232 kcal — internally consistent. GrabFood is not used as a nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the authoritative nutrient basis.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Tofu (soybean curd) GI approximately 15; composite dish adjusted upward to 35 to account for pork floss added sugars and deep-frying.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain tofu has a published GI of approximately 15 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Deep-fried tofu with pork floss is estimated at GI 35 (low category) to account for the modest carbohydrate contribution from pork floss which may contain added sugar and any light coating from deep-frying. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 20 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 380 mg/100 g from pork floss and soy sauce; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, representing 41–63% of the recommended daily limit of 1500–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; if consumed, restrict to a small portion of 100 g or less and request low-sodium soy sauce, as pork floss is a high-sodium processed meat that substantially elevates total sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI of approximately 35 and modest carbohydrate of 8 g/100 g are favourable; however, deep-frying increases caloric density to 232 kcal/100 g and pork floss may contain added sugars that raise postprandial glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in controlled portions of 150 g or less, pairing it with non-starchy vegetables to moderate glycaemic response, while verifying that pork floss is free of added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying elevates total fat to 16 g/100 g with 3.2 g saturated fat and 0.2 g trans fat; combined with high sodium of 380 mg/100 g and cholesterol of 28 mg/100 g, this dish presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish due to its high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium content; occasional small portions of 100 g or less may be acceptable within a broader heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; pork floss as a processed red meat contributes arachidonic acid and high sodium, compounding pro-inflammatory effects.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; deep-frying is a hard disqualifier due to AGE formation, and the processed pork component further amplifies inflammatory pathways.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Tofu with Pork Floss (Vietnamese: Đậu Hũ Lướt Vân; romanized: Dau Hu Luot Van; Chinese: 炸豆腐配肉松) is a popular Vietnamese appetizer or side dish consisting of firm tofu blocks that are deep-fried until golden and crispy, then generously topped with savoury, fluffy pork floss (bông thịt heo). The dish is widely served in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore. The exterior of the tofu is crunchy and oil-rich, while the interior remains soft and silken. Pork floss adds a salty, slightly sweet, umami-rich contrast in both texture and flavour. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 232 kcal, 14 g protein, 16 g fat (3.2 g saturated, 0.2 g trans), 8 g carbohydrate, 2 g sugar, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 380 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making carbohydrate impact modest, but the high sodium from pork floss and soy sauce raises concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making this dish unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. A standard plate serving is approximately 250 g providing around 580 kcal. Soy is a primary allergen; gluten may be present via soy sauce. Pork floss is not halal.
common_user_queries: Is deep-fried tofu with pork floss healthy?
How many calories in Dau Hu Luot Van?

Is Dau Hu Luot Van gluten-free?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried tofu and dried/cured pork, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. The input GrabFood values (confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with the USDA composite estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on published tofu GI data adjusted for pork floss carbohydrate contribution.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite deep-fried tofu and pork floss dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single composite entry as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible for deep-fried tofu with pork floss; no significant vitamin C source present in ingredients — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central fried tofu SR Legacy entry. trans_fat_g is 0.2 g/100 g as reported in input data, consistent with minor trans fat formation during deep-frying. Pork floss may contain added sugar; consumers should verify product label. Halal status is not confirmed as pork floss is a pork-derived product. Vietnamese dish served in Singapore context. Malay local name provided as descriptive translation: tauhu goreng dengan serunding daging babi. Tamil local name provided as descriptive translation. Hindi local name provided as descriptive translation. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases array. G6 multilingual compliance: Vietnamese cuisine requires Vietnamese local name (satisfied: Đậu Hũ Lướt Vân); dish is served in Singapore context so Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases have been added to satisfy G6 and Q8 requirements for Singapore-served dishes.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Deep-Fried Tofu with Pork Floss' is precise and unambiguous. Vietnamese local name 'Đậu Hũ Lướt Vân' present. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names added for Singapore-served dish context. G6 multilingual requirement satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 232.0, protein_g 14.0, fat_g 16.0, carbohydrate_g 8.0 all present and numeric. Energy cross-check passes (14×4 + 16×9 + 8×4 = 232 kcal). Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate — approved source. GrabFood explicitly excluded as nutrient source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 1.2, sodium_mg 380.0, saturated_fat_g 3.2, sugar_g 2.0 — all present as numeric values, plausible for deep-fried tofu with pork floss.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 35 (low), gi_category 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving 7.0, gi_source documented with University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal. Analogous estimate basis clearly stated.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI for fried tofu food product provided with closest_parent match type and gap note. FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Tofu, fried) provided with closest_parent match type and gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly with machine key type2_diabetes.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 14 entries covering English variants, Vietnamese native script, romanized form, Chinese script forms, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi forms. aliases_by_language populated for english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, vietnamese, and romanized. All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. Mandatory language alias coverage satisfied.

G6 fixed by adding Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names for Singapore-served dish context. Q8 fixed by adding corresponding aliases for all mandatory languages. Vietnamese multilingual requirement satisfied. All aliases consistent between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array. Three required health conditions present with clinical specificity. Ontology anchors honest with closest_parent match types documented.
